meta.place: African Journal of Technology and Science

text.heading: SEPARATION OF CASHEW NUT SHELL LIQUID BY COLUMN CHROMATOGRAPHY
INTRODUCTION
text.p: Cashew nut shell liquid (CNSL) is a natural product with bitter taste and dark brownish colour. It is a caustic phenolic oil. CNSL is obtained from cashew nuts either by roasting, extraction in hot oil or in a solvent; or by mechanical expression of the oil from the shell of cashew nuts. CNSL is essentially a mixture of 4 phenolic compounds namely anacardic acid, cardol, cardanol and 2-methlycardol (Fig. 1). These natural products could serve as alternative source of phenolic compounds hitherto sourced from petrochemicals. CNSL has found uses in areas as diverse as in the manufacture of brake lining of automobiles, manufacturing of heat and waterproof paints, corrosion resistant varnishes, and insulating enamels for the electrical industry. Ohler (1979) reported that fresh CNSL contains about 90% by weight anacardic acid, a derivative of o-carboxyphenol that readily decarboxylates on heating and converts to cardanol. Cardanol is the components that has the properties that are responsible for the aforementioned applications of CNSL. The remaining 10% of CNSL consists of cardol, a resorcinol that is mainly responsible for the vesicative activity of the CNSL. Ohler (1979) further stated that the actual composition of CNSL, colour and stability depend to some extent on the duration and condition of storage as well as on the method of its production and the nature of heat applied. Column chromatography is a method that can be used for the separation of liquid mixtures even when they have similar solubilities in a particular solvent. Usually a glass column about 30 cm long and 2 cm in diameter, which narrows at one end to an outlet, is normally used in the laboratory (Norman and Waddington, 1983). Hamilton and Sewell (1982) reported that in adsorption (column) chromatography, separation is the consequence of polar interactions between the active groups on the stationary phase and polar or polarizable functional groups on the solute molecules. CNSL was made into a cake by first reducing its viscosity with the addition of chloroform, then silica gel was added to it and was allowed to cake in an oven. The CNSL cake was then fed to the column and the solvent was allowed to drip into the column to effect the separation of CNSL into its constituents. The objectives of this study are to separate the CNSL in the cake to its constituents; determine the RF and RM-values of each constituent; and to recover cardanol, which is the most useful component for its synthesis into a cation-exchange resin.
text.heading: MATERIALS AND METHODS
MATERIALS
text.p: Cashew nut shell liquid was obtained from ACET Nig. Ltd., Owo, Nigeria. Benzene, chloroform and silical gel for chromatographic analysis of particle size 0.13 - 0.25mm (60 - 120 mesh) are of analytical grade and were obtained from BDH Laboratory Supply, England.
text.heading: METHODS
Preparation of CNSL cake
text.p: 80 mL of CNSL were dissolved in 60 mL of chloroform to make it less viscous. Then 170 g of silica gel (60 - 120 mesh) were added continuously until the solution became paste-like. The paste was dried in the oven at a temperature of 110 ± 1 0C for three hours to obtain the CNSL cake. Drying of the CNSL cake in the oven simultaneously effected the decarboxylation of anacardic acid to cardanol.
text.heading: Separation of CNSL
text.p: A 50 cm3 burette was used as the column. The mobile phase was equal portions of benzene and chloroform. All glassware used including beakers, separating funnel and glass rod were washed with chromic acid, rinsed with distilled water and dried. Cotton wool was inserted into the bottom of column down to the constricted end with a glass rod. Then 37 g of silica gel were poured into the column while tapping the column. The silica gel was wetted by adding the mobile phase into the column through a separating funnel clamped on a stand above the burette. As the mobile phase passed through the silica gel packing, the void volume was noted when the first drop of mobile phase from the burrete appeared at the outlet.

A second cotton wool was placed on top of the wetted silica gel to prevent any disturbance of the column when the CNSL cake was added. CNSL cake was then added to the remaining part of the column leaving a little space above for the mobile phase dropping from the separating funnel.

As soon as the cake was introduced to the column and the mobile phase was added, the process of separation began. The components were eluted at different rates by the mobile phase. The first eluant appeared very fast, it came out within one hour of commencement of separation.

The second and third eluants (components) were held to the stationary phase for much longer periods about 18 and 28 hours respectively. The components (eluants) were collected in different beakers and the mobile phase was allowed to evaporate at room temperature. The RF-value, the RM-value, yield and percentage composition of each component were then determined. Three runs were made to check the reproducibility of the results. More detailed procedure is available elsewhere (Oghome, 2003). Frais (1972) stated that compounds may be catalogued by their RF-value. The RF-value of a component is constant under given experimental conditions such as mobile phase, stationary phase, temperature, pH and pressure.
text.heading: Column Chromatography
text.p: In column chromatography, a sample is introduced at the top of the column and subsequent development by the mobile phase may eventually elute the separated components from the column in order of their polarity, the less polar ones first. Each component can be identified if the RF-value can be calculated. In transversing a length of column, a volume of the mobile phase equal to the volume of the voids (Vi) in that length will be used. If then, a volume Vx is used before a given component is eluted, the mobile phase front will have moved a distance equal to the column length x Vx / Vi. The RF of that component can therefore be calculated from the expression (Heald and Smith, 1974):
The RM value reflects the affinity of the solute for the stationary phase and is defined by
The greater the RM value, the more polar the substance. Thus the more polar a species, the slower will it be moved along a developing chromatogram.
text.heading: Determination of Acid Number
text.p: The raw CNSL and the pure cardanol were analyzed for their acid number. 2.03 g of cardanol and 2.04 g of CNSL were weighed into two separate 250 mL Erlenmeyer flasks and 100 mL of ethanol were added to each flask. 0.5 mL of methyl orange was added as an indicator. The mixture was shaken until the sample was completely dissolved in the ethanol solvent. The mixture was then titrated against a standardized 0.1 N KOH solution.
The acid number was calculated using the following expression:
text.heading: RESULTS AND DISCUSSIONS
text.p: The three main components of CNSL after carboxylation achieved by heating the CNSL cake in the oven at 110 0C which are cardanol, cardol and 2-methylcardol were found to be all soluble in the mobile phase which was a mixture of equal portions of benzene and chloroform. However, when contacted with the silica gel adsorbent (the stationary phase), the solutes (components) showed different affinities for the stationary phase and were therefore separated into three bands in the chromatographic column before each solute (component) was eluted.

The RF-value for each component was determined from the volume of solvent used in eluting it using Eq(1) for each of the three experimental runs. The mean RF-value and the standard deviation for each component were calculated. The mean RM-values for each component was then calculated using equation 2. The mean RF and RM values for each component are shown in Table 1.

The RM-value reflects the affinity of the solute (component) for the stationary phase, the greater the RM value, the more polar a component was and the slower will it be moved along the chromatographic column. In

this study, the component with the highest RM-value of 0.760 was 2-methylcardol. It was the component that was eluted last and it took about 28 h. to elute it. This was followed by cardol (RM = 0.679), cardol elution took 18 h.
The least polar component cardanol (RM = -0.028) was eluted in less than 1 h. Cardanol being the component of interest could therefore be easily separated by this column chromatographic technique.

The mean value of the yield, and the standard deviation of each component are shown in Table 1. The mean value of yield was used to calculated the percentage composition of the decarboxylated CNSL. The result showed that the largest proportion of the decarboxylated CNSL was cardanol. The percentage composition obtained in this study agreed with those of Ohler (1979), even though the composition of CNSL components is known to vary naturally with the plant species and may also be affected by the method of the extraction of the oil from the cashew nuts. The average quantity of cardanol recovered from the initial 80 mL of raw CNSL was 53.56 g.
The pH of CNSL and cardanol were determined to be 6.69 and 3.43 respectively. The result shows that cardanol is more acidic than the raw CNSL.
The acid number of CNSL and cardanol were determined to be 13.68 and 16.60 mg of KOH / g respectively. These values confirm the more acidic nature of cardanol to CNSL.
text.heading: CONCLUSION
text.p: The separation of CNSL a natural and renewable product into cardanol, cardol and 2-methycardol using column chromatography has been reported in this study. The study revealed that cardanol had the smallest RM - value and so had more affinity for the mobile phase than the adsorbent (stationary phase) hence it was separated easily from CNSL. In addition, the percentage composition by weight of the CNSL used were found to be 92.1 (cardanol), 6.3 (cardol) and 1.6 (2-methylcardol) . A reasonable conclusion therefore is that cardanol can be recovered easily and at a low cost from CNSL using column chromatography and an appropriate mobile phase such as a mixture of equal portions of benzene and chloroform with silica gel as the stationary phase. The RF-value of cardanol obtained in this study can be used in the design of an industrial chromatographic column for its separation from CNSL.
